name: Experiment Intent
summary: Declarative variant-assignment: 'this subtree renders the arm assigned to the current unit'. Ratified #1414 Fork 1. The variant dimension is OPEN — the arm set is author-defined (A/B, multivariate) — with the most-permissive default = the control/default arm (OpenFeature DEFAULT), so a subtree with no live experiment renders control. UX-only: which arm shows is the concern here; HOW a unit is bucketed is impl behind a swappable evaluation provider (the DISTINCT @webeverything/contracts/experiment seam, reusing the Guard PATTERN, returning {value, variant, reason} in OpenFeature vocab, with NO security semantics — a variant arm is not an authz verdict). A BOOLEAN feature flag is the separate access-control gate (#1481), not this intent. Resolving an arm EMITS an Experiment Exposed event (experimentId / variant / subjectId) through the analytics intent's track() sink — per #1414 Fork 3 / #1415 an exposure IS a track() composing the existing emission seam (#1476 vocabulary entry), NOT a second transport; the exposure fires AT arm resolution, which structurally precedes any downstream effect measurement, so the ordering guarantee needs no contract beyond #1415's emit (#1480).

variant.description: Which arm of the experiment renders. An OPEN dimension — the arm set is author-defined per experiment (a 2-arm A/B, or multivariate control/treatment-a/treatment-b/…); the values below are only the canonical example. Most-permissive default: the control/default arm (OpenFeature DEFAULT), rendered when no live assignment resolves.

description:
What This Is

Declarative variant assignment (ratified #1414 Fork 1): a subtree declares that it renders the arm assigned to the current unit, and the assigned arm is resolved by a swappable provider. The variant dimension is open — the arm set is author-defined (A/B or multivariate) — and the most-permissive default is the control / default arm (OpenFeature DEFAULT): a subtree with no live experiment renders control, never breaks.

UX-only — the mechanic lives behind a provider

This intent is UX-only: which arm shows is its concern; how a unit is bucketed (hash-bucket, server SDK, sticky override) is impl behind a distinct evaluation-provider contract (@webeverything/contracts/experiment) that reuses the Guard seam pattern — native-first default → project override → custom plug. The provider returns { value, variant, reason } (the OpenFeature evaluation-details vocabulary, borrowed verbatim), and its evaluate is async + a UX mirror, never the source of truth.

Not an authorization verdict

A variant arm carries no security semantics — it is a pick-one-of-N rendering choice, not an allow/deny decision. A boolean feature flag (one-of-two where one arm is "off") IS the access-control gate on the Guard provider seam (access-control, #1481); a multivariate flag is this experiment intent. Same provider shape, different outcome families and trust boundaries — OpenFeature's own typed-flag distinction (boolean = gate, string/object = selector) is the upstream precedent.

Exposure telemetry — composes the analytics sink, not a new transport

Resolving an arm emits an Experiment Exposed event (experimentId / variant / subjectId) — the record that this unit saw this arm, the join key every downstream metric needs. Per #1414 Fork 3 / #1415, an exposure IS a track(): it flows through the analytics intent's existing swappable emission seam as a standard Experiment Exposed event (#1476 vocabulary entry on the Analytics Event Vocabulary), never a second transport — mirroring OpenFeature's Tracking ⊥ Evaluation split (the provider evaluates; the sink delivers).

Ordering invariant — fixed, no extra contract

The exposure fires at the moment of arm resolution (when the evaluation provider returns { value, variant, reason }), which by construction precedes any downstream effect/metric attributed to the arm — so the "exposure before measurement" guarantee is a fixed structural invariant of the emission point, requiring no contract beyond #1415's emit (#1480). De-duplication of repeat exposures within a session is an emission-seam concern (the sink's policy), not an intent surface.
